title: California AI Legislation — Active Laws and Key Bills
official_title: California artificial intelligence legislation: enacted laws and significant pending bills as of 2025
overview: California has the most active AI legislative pipeline of any US state, reflecting its role as home to most major AI developers. Several bills were enacted in 2024; the high-profile SB 1047 (AI safety for frontier models) was vetoed by Governor Newsom but significant other laws took effect. California's AI legal landscape now spans transparency obligations, training data disclosure, deepfake restrictions, employment protections, and healthcare AI rules. Companies based in or serving California users face a growing and fragmented set of obligations.

enacted_laws.title: AI Training Data Transparency Act
enacted_laws.citation: California Business and Professions Code § 22756 et seq.
enacted_laws.overview: Requires developers of AI systems trained on data to post on their website the general categories of data used to train the system, including whether personal information was used, and whether synthetic data was used.
key_obligations: Post training data transparency documentation on developer website before system deployment
Include: general categories of data, whether personal information is included, whether synthetic data is included, data vintage (approximate dates of data collection)
Update disclosure within 60 days of material changes to training data
enacted_laws.applies_to: Developers of generative AI systems and large language models offered to California users
enacted_laws.penalties: Injunctive relief; no specified per-violation penalty

enacted_laws.title: California AI Transparency Act
enacted_laws.citation: California Business and Professions Code § 22756.5 et seq.
enacted_laws.overview: Requires large AI system providers to make detection tools available and disclose when content is AI-generated. Targets providers with over 1 million monthly users in California.
key_obligations: Provide a free publicly available AI detection tool that can identify content generated by the system
Include AI disclosure in generated content through manifest disclosure (visible watermark, label) or latent disclosure (metadata)
Disclose in terms of service that the system can generate synthetic content
Provide a mechanism for users to report AI-generated content without disclosure
enacted_laws.threshold: AI systems with more than 1 million monthly users in California
enacted_laws.penalties: Civil penalties up to $5,000 per violation; Attorney General enforcement

enacted_laws.title: Digital Replica Consent — Performers
enacted_laws.overview: Requires informed consent before creating or using a digital replica of a performer's voice or likeness using AI. Applies to entertainment industry contracts.
key_obligations: Obtain written consent before using AI to create a digital replica of a performer
Consent must be specific — cannot be buried in general contract terms
Performers retain the right to have digital replicas removed
enacted_laws.applies_to: Entertainment industry companies using performer digital replicas

enacted_laws.title: Safe and Secure Innovation for Frontier Artificial Intelligence Models Act
enacted_laws.overview: Would have required developers of large AI models (trained on >$100M of compute) to implement safety protocols, conduct pre-deployment testing, and maintain kill-switch capability. Vetoed on grounds of over-breadth and potential chilling effect on California AI industry.
enacted_laws.significance: Despite veto, signals California legislative intent around frontier AI safety. A revised version is expected in 2025-26 legislative session. Companies should monitor.
key_provisions_if_enacted: Pre-deployment safety evaluation for models above compute threshold
Hazard analysis covering CBRN risks and cybersecurity
Ability to shut down or modify model remotely
Incident reporting to California Attorney General

enacted_laws.title: Deceptive AI Content in Elections
enacted_laws.overview: Prohibits distributing materially deceptive AI-generated content of political candidates or elected officials within 120 days of an election without disclosure.
key_obligations: AI-generated content of political figures in ads must include clear disclosure
Platforms must provide mechanisms to label AI-generated political content
Election authorities must be notified of covered AI-generated content

pending_significant_bills_2025.title: AI Incident Reporting
pending_significant_bills_2025.overview: Would require developers and deployers of AI systems to report certain AI incidents involving death, serious injury, or critical infrastructure disruption to a state agency.

pending_significant_bills_2025.title: Personal Information in AI Training Data
pending_significant_bills_2025.overview: Would extend California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) rights to cover personal information used in AI training. Individuals could opt out of their data being used to train AI models.

ccpa_ai_intersection.applies_to: Businesses meeting CCPA thresholds that process personal information to train AI systems
key_rights: Right to know whether personal information is used to train AI
Right to opt out of sale of personal information (which may include training data licensing)
Right to deletion of personal information — extending to training data removal where technically feasible
ccpa_ai_intersection.note: CCPA does not explicitly address AI training but existing rights apply. CPPA rulemaking on AI and automated decision-making is ongoing.

cross_references.relevance: All California AI processing of personal data is subject to CCPA/CPRA. Automated decision-making rulemaking is pending from the California Privacy Protection Agency.
California AI transparency requirements (AB 2013, SB 942) overlap with EU AI Act transparency obligations but use different thresholds and mechanisms.
